topics: Story of a lazy beggar and a rich man
Sanskrit subhashita on laziness and hard work
Dvitiya Vibhakti (second case ending) in Sanskrit grammar
subject: Sanskrit

sections.heading: 🧙 The Quest Begins — A Lazy Hero Who Could Change!
sections.body: Our story is about a young, healthy man who lived in a big village. Even though he had a strong body, he chose to beg every day instead of working. He would walk around asking people for money. Some people gave him money, and some scolded him. Just like in cricket — imagine a fit, strong player sitting on the bench every match saying 'I don't want to play!' — that is our beggar!
keyPoints: The beggar was young and strong — he had the power to work, but he chose laziness instead
Laziness is like a dragon inside us that stops us from using our true powers

sections.heading: ⚔️ The Rich Man's Magical Offer — A Spell of Wisdom
sections.body: One day, a rich man came on that same road. The beggar asked him for lots of money. The rich man said — 'Give me your feet, I will give you 5000 rupees!' The beggar said, 'Without feet, how will I walk?' Then the rich man asked for his hands. The beggar refused again. Every time the rich man offered money in exchange for a body part, the beggar said NO! The rich man was casting a spell of wisdom — he was trying to show the beggar that his body itself is worth more than any treasure!
keyPoints: The rich man showed the beggar that his own body parts are his greatest treasure — worth thousands of rupees
If you have a healthy body, you already have the power to earn your own money through hard work

sections.heading: 🐉 Defeating the Dragon — Laziness is the Real Enemy!
sections.body: The rich man told the beggar a powerful truth — 'Laziness is the biggest enemy living inside your own body!' Just like during Diwali, if you are too lazy to light even one diya, the whole house stays dark — laziness keeps your whole life dark! The rich man said hard work is your best friend, like your best teammate in a kho-kho game. After hearing all this, the beggar finally understood. He left begging, worked hard, and lived a happy life!
keyPoints: आलस्यं हि मनुष्याणां शरीरस्थो महान् रिपुः — Laziness is the greatest enemy living inside us
Hard work (परिश्रम) is your true friend — it never lets you feel sad or fail in life

sections.heading: 📜 The Sanskrit Subhashita — A Wizard's Ancient Spell of Truth
sections.body: This chapter teaches us a famous Sanskrit saying called a Subhashita — it means 'wise words'. It says: Laziness is the biggest enemy inside a person's body. And there is no better friend than hard work — because whoever works hard never feels sad! Think of it like this — in a game of kabaddi, the player who keeps trying, running, and touching the line always wins. The one who sits down loses the game of life!
keyPoints: उद्यमसमः बन्धुः — There is no friend equal to hard work (उद्यम means effort and hard work)
This subhashita is a powerful spell every young hero must memorise for their life quest!

sections.heading: 🔮 Grammar Power — Dvitiya Vibhakti (Second Case Ending)
sections.body: Now for your grammar quest! In Sanskrit, when something is the object of an action — like 'Ram reads the book' — the word 'book' gets a special form called Dvitiya Vibhakti. For example, पुस्तक becomes पुस्तकम् (one book), पुस्तके (two books), and पुस्तकानि (many books). It is like how in Hindi we say 'किताब पढ़ता है' — the object gets pointed out. This chapter practises these forms for boy-words (पुल्लिंग), girl-words (स्त्रीलिंग), and neutral words (नपुंसकलिंग)!
keyPoints: Dvitiya Vibhakti is used for the object of the action — singular ends in म् (ग्रामम्), dual ends in औ (ग्रामौ), plural ends in अन् (ग्रामान्)
This rule works for all three genders — practice with words like भिक्षाम्, नदीम्, फलम् to master this grammar spell!

summary: Chapter 14 takes us on a brave quest through the story of a lazy but healthy beggar who meets a wise rich man. The rich man uses a clever trick — offering money for the beggar's body parts — to show that the beggar's own healthy body is his greatest treasure. The real dragon in this story is laziness (आलस्य), which lives inside us and stops us from achieving great things. The chapter teaches us the powerful Sanskrit subhashita: laziness is our biggest enemy, and hard work is our best friend. After understanding this wisdom, the beggar defeats his inner dragon, gives up begging, and builds a happy life through effort. On the grammar side, young wizards learn Dvitiya Vibhakti — the second case ending in Sanskrit — which tells us how to change a word when it is the object of an action, across all three genders and three numbers. Master this spell and your Sanskrit realm will grow stronger every day!
